---
title: "Exercises: Vectors and Linear Combinations"
description: "Practice problems and worked solutions for vectors, span, dependence, and column combinations."
page-type: exercise
status: published
update-status: current
level: foundation
topic: linear-algebra
prerequisites:
  - /topics/foundation/linear-algebra/concepts/vectors-and-linear-combinations.qmd
learning-goals:
  - "Practice writing vectors as combinations of generators."
  - "Recognize when vectors are dependent or independent."
  - "Translate matrix-vector multiplication into column-space language."
applications:
  - machine-learning
  - signal-processing
keywords:
  - exercises
  - vectors
  - span
  - linear combinations
maintainer: mtuann
last-reviewed: 2026-04-24
source-check-date: 2026-04-24
review-cadence: annual
date-modified: last-modified
draft: false
related-pages:
  - /topics/foundation/linear-algebra/proofs/span-is-a-subspace.qmd
  - /topics/foundation/linear-algebra/labs/linear-combinations-and-span-geometry.qmd
---

## Scope and Goals

This set trains four things:

- writing vectors explicitly as linear combinations
- checking span and dependence
- reading matrix-vector multiplication by columns
- preparing for basis and dimension

## Prerequisites

- vector addition and scalar multiplication
- the definition of span
- the idea that $Ax$ is a column combination

## Warm-Up Problems

1. Write

   $$
   \begin{bmatrix}
   4 \\
   -1
   \end{bmatrix}
   $$

   as a linear combination of the standard basis vectors in $\mathbb{R}^2$.
2. Let

   $$
   v_1 =
   \begin{bmatrix}
   1 \\
   1
   \end{bmatrix},
   \qquad
   v_2 =
   \begin{bmatrix}
   1 \\
   -1
   \end{bmatrix}.
   $$

   Find coefficients $a,b$ so that $av_1+bv_2 = (3,1)^\top$.

## Core Problems

1. Decide whether

   $$
   b =
   \begin{bmatrix}
   2 \\
   5
   \end{bmatrix}
   $$

   belongs to the span of

   $$
   v_1 =
   \begin{bmatrix}
   1 \\
   2
   \end{bmatrix},
   \qquad
   v_2 =
   \begin{bmatrix}
   1 \\
   1
   \end{bmatrix}.
   $$

2. Let

   $$
   A =
   \begin{bmatrix}
   1 & 0 & 1 \\
   0 & 1 & 1
   \end{bmatrix},
   \qquad
   x =
   \begin{bmatrix}
   2 \\
   -1 \\
   3
   \end{bmatrix}.
   $$

   Compute $Ax$ and explain it as a linear combination of the columns of $A$.
3. Show that

   $$
   v_3 =
   \begin{bmatrix}
   2 \\
   0
   \end{bmatrix}
   $$

   is redundant in the set

   $$
   \left\{
   \begin{bmatrix}
   1 \\
   0
   \end{bmatrix},
   \begin{bmatrix}
   0 \\
   1
   \end{bmatrix},
   \begin{bmatrix}
   2 \\
   0
   \end{bmatrix}
   \right\}.
   $$

## Proof Problems

1. Prove that if $u$ and $v$ are both in $\operatorname{span}\{w_1,\dots,w_k\}$, then $u+v$ is also in that span.
2. Prove that if one vector in a spanning set is itself a linear combination of the others, then removing it does not change the span.

## Computational or Applied Problems

1. Open [Computation Lab: Linear Combinations and Span Geometry](../labs/linear-combinations-and-span-geometry.qmd). Move the angle close to `0` degrees and record what happens to the determinant and the span description.
2. In software, create a small matrix with dependent columns and test two different coefficient vectors that give the same output.

## Hints

1. To check membership in a span, solve for the coefficients.
2. For matrix-vector multiplication, read each component of $x$ as a weight on a column of $A$.
3. Redundancy means one direction is already available from the others.

## Full Solutions

### Solution 1

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
4 \\
-1
\end{bmatrix}
=
4
\begin{bmatrix}
1 \\
0
\end{bmatrix}
-
\begin{bmatrix}
0 \\
1
\end{bmatrix}.
$$

### Solution 2

We solve

$$
a
\begin{bmatrix}
1 \\
1
\end{bmatrix}
+
b
\begin{bmatrix}
1 \\
-1
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
3 \\
1
\end{bmatrix}.
$$

This gives

$$
a+b=3,
\qquad
a-b=1.
$$

So $a=2$ and $b=1$.

### Solution 3

Solve

$$
a
\begin{bmatrix}
1 \\
2
\end{bmatrix}
+
b
\begin{bmatrix}
1 \\
1
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
2 \\
5
\end{bmatrix}.
$$

From the first coordinate, $a+b=2$. From the second, $2a+b=5$. Subtracting gives $a=3$, so $b=-1$.

Therefore $b$ belongs to the span.

### Solution 4

The columns of $A$ are

$$
a_1 =
\begin{bmatrix}
1 \\
0
\end{bmatrix},
\qquad
a_2 =
\begin{bmatrix}
0 \\
1
\end{bmatrix},
\qquad
a_3 =
\begin{bmatrix}
1 \\
1
\end{bmatrix}.
$$

So

$$
Ax = 2a_1 - a_2 + 3a_3
=
2
\begin{bmatrix}
1 \\
0
\end{bmatrix}
-
\begin{bmatrix}
0 \\
1
\end{bmatrix}
+
3
\begin{bmatrix}
1 \\
1
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
5 \\
2
\end{bmatrix}.
$$

### Solution 5

Because

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
2 \\
0
\end{bmatrix}
=
2
\begin{bmatrix}
1 \\
0
\end{bmatrix},
$$

the third vector is already in the span of the first. Removing it does not change the span.

## Common Errors

- treating coefficients as if they must be unique even when the generators are dependent
- checking span by eye instead of solving for coefficients
- forgetting that matrix-vector multiplication is column-based, not row-based, in this interpretation

## What To Do Next

Open [Matrices and Linear Maps](../concepts/matrices-and-linear-maps.qmd) next. That page explains why these same linear combinations become operator language once matrices are viewed as maps.

## Sources and Further Reading

- [MIT 18.06SC: Basis and Dimension](https://live.ocw.mit.edu/courses/18-06sc-linear-algebra-fall-2011/resources/basis-and-dimension-1/) - `First pass` - good official problem context for span and dependence. Checked `2026-04-24`.
- [Hefferon, Linear Algebra](https://hefferon.net/linearalgebra/) - `Second pass` - strong extra exercises for span, dependence, and column space. Checked `2026-04-24`.
- [Attention is All you Need](https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2017/hash/3f5ee243547dee91fbd053c1c4a845aa-Abstract.html) - `Paper bridge` - useful later, once weighted vector combinations feel natural. Checked `2026-04-24`.
